Jehoram hated his brothers and killed them, for the same reason that Cain hated Abel and killed him, because their righteousness condemned his wickedness. In the mystery of God’s providence such men sometimes prosper for a time; but the Lord has righteous purposes in allowing such events, some of which may be understood now, and the rest will be seen later.

A warning from God was sent to Jehoram. The Spirit of prophecy might direct Elijah to prepare this writing knowing about Jehoram's crimes beforehand. He is plainly told that his sin will certainly ruin him. But it is not surprising that sinners are not frightened away from sin and toward repentance by the threats of misery in another world, when the certainty of misery in this world, the loss of their possessions, and the destruction of their health, will not restrain them from immoral behavior. See Jehoram here stripped of all his comforts. Thus God plainly showed that the conflict was with him and his household. He had killed all his brothers to strengthen himself; now, all his sons are killed except one. David's house must not be completely destroyed, like those of Israel’s kings, because there was a blessing in it: that of the Messiah. Good men may suffer from diseases; but for them they are fatherly corrections, and by the support of God’s comfort the soul can dwell at peace even when the body is in pain. To be sick and poor, sick and alone, but especially to be sick and in sin, sick and under God's curse, sick and without grace to bear it, is a most miserable situation. Wickedness and irreverence make men despicable, even in the eyes of those who have little religion.
